EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Dolly that moved inland in the Lower Rio Grande Valley of south Texas, tracked around a large subtropical high centered over north Texas and was drifting across north Georgia.  The deep tropical moisture from the remnants of the hurricane combined with cold air aloft in an active northwest flow on the east side of the southern plains subtropical ridge to result in the development of numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  While the moist adiabatic atmosphere keep most thunderstorms below severe limits, flash flooding in the southwest part of the Atlanta metropolitan area became an issue during the evening as thunderstorms trained over the same areas for several hours.  Four to five inches of rain in Fulton and Douglas county southwest of Atlanta was common during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the hydrologist at the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia and information obtained from the Fulton County Emergency Management Director concluded that flash flooding had occurred along and near Bear Creek at Campbellton-Redwine Road in south Fulton county, roughly seven miles northwest of Palmetto.  This was the result of persistent thunderstorms that trained over the area during the evening hours.  Rainfall of 3 to 5 inches fell across this area between 6 pm and midnight with two rounds of significant thunderstorms during this period.  Significant water and mud erosion was observed along Campbellton-Redwine Road roughly 0.5 mile northwest of Bear Creek and another area of erosion noted along Woodruff Road approximately 0.2 mile west of Bear Creek.  Bear Creek was estimated to have risen around two feet above bankful during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front was located across north Georgia.  A weak trough was moving from the Ohio Valley into the mid-Atlantic and southeast United States.  A strong Bermuda high was present off the southeast United States coast.  This was keeping a warm, moist, and unstable air mass over the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed across mainly central Georgia where moisture content was higher and the air mass was more unstable.  A few of these became severe, with hail being the primary element.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail in Wrens.  Radar supports that large hail of similar size likely fell across a good portion of northeast Jefferson county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was deepening over the Ohio Valley.  High pressure aloft over the southeast United States was weakening and shifting west into the southern plains.  The atmosphere across north and central Georgia became very unstable during the afternoon as temperatures soared into the mid 90s with dewpoints in the low and mid 70s.  This combined with weak mesoscale boundaries set the stage for scattered to numerous thunderstorms to develop by mid-afternoon.  The hot temperatures allowed the activity to continue well into the evening and beyond sunset.  The activity initially began around the periphery of the upper high to the west in east Georgia then spread southward from Tennessee into north Georgia later and continued propagating southward during the evening hours.  Several downbursts were reported resulting in a number of downed trees and power lines, especially across north and east Georgia.  A few marginal severe hail events were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sautee Cooperative Observer conducted a damage survey across the north and east part of the county and found three large trees down, two down on the highway just east of Sautee on Georgia Highway 17 and another along Georgia Highway 255 east of Stovall Mill.  Several tree limbs and branches were also down between Robertstown, Cleveland, and areas east of Sautee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front lingered off the Georgia coast while another rare July cold front approached from the northwest aided by northwest flow aloft. With the typical July heat and humidity in place, a disturbance in the northwest flow aloft helped to enhance the development of afternoon thunderstorms.  The activity of significance was generally confined to areas near the Alabama-Georgia border, with little activity noted in east Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel-sized hail.  Estimated wind gusts of 40 mph also blew down some trees in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front for mid-summer was moving into northwest Georgia supported by a disturbance in northwest flow aloft.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in two to three waves ahead of the front, starting early in the day and continuing through afternoon.  However, extensive cloud cover early in the day limited the severity of the thunderstorms.  Only one storm was reported to have been severe in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that a home was struck and set on fire by lightning.  A specific damage amount was not provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damage to trees, power lines, and several structures across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across the northern half of the county.  An old barn was destroyed.  Straight line wind damage was confined within this storm.  In the northeast part of the county near Patten,  two sheds were destroyed.  One small grain silo was destroyed, with the debris strewn 200 yards.  Phone lines were knocked out, with 200 homes without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low continued to deepen across the northeast U.S. and was digging southward into the southeast United States  A strong cold front was moving south from the Ohio Valley toward the southeast United States.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in Alabama during the early afternoon and expanded and spread eastward into western Georgia during the mid-afternoon.  Many of these storms produced damaging wind and large hail.  An expansive outflow boundary spread eastward across the state in advance of the thunderstorms causing additional thunderstorms to develop further eastward.  However, thunderstorms decreased in coverage as they spread further east and all of the activity had ended by 10 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County 911 Center reported that lightning struck a tree on Bell Avenue causing it to fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced several reports of wind damage and a tornado across portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same supercell thunderstorm which spawned the brief EF0 tornado in southeast Decatur County produced an EF1 tornado just west of State Highway 93 on Courtney Lane.  Mobile homes along Courtney Lane suffered minor roof damage and skirting was torn from their bases.  Also, numerous trees were snapped or uprooted.  The tornado traveled northeast and uprooted several trees near two homes on Shorty Lane.  A mobile home lost portions of its roof.  A single family home lost a few shingles off its roof.  Two homes east of Shorty Lane on Wilcox Road were damaged.  The tornado then moved along Pine Forest Road east of State Highway 111.  About a mile up Pine Forest Road, a mobile home was destroyed with the free standing carport tossed 200 yards into the nearby forest.  The occupant of the mobile home was injured when it rolled over.  The roof and exterior walls were destroyed.  The frame remained attached to the undercarriage of the mobile home.

Northwest flow aloft was intensifying across the region.  Thunderstorms were less numerous than on the 22nd and more concentrated mostly across the central and west central areas.  One thunderstorm in Macon county produced a strong downburst which resulted in at least 50 downed trees and dozens of power lines.  At least 10 homes were damaged by this isolated thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heard County 911 Center reported nickel-sized hail in the far southwest corner of the county in the Glenn area.

One thunderstorm in Macon county produced a strong downburst which resulted in at least 50 downed trees and dozens of power lines.  At least 10 homes were damaged by this isolated thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deputy with the Macon County Sheriff's Office reported that from 50 to 75 trees were down and hundreds more tree limbs in and around Marshallville.  Dozens of power lines were also down in the area.  At least 10 homes sustained damage from downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front for mid-summer was moving into northwest Georgia supported by a disturbance in northwest flow aloft.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in two to three waves ahead of the front, starting early in the day and continuing through afternoon.  However, extensive cloud cover early in the day limited the severity of the thunderstorms.  Only one storm was reported to have been severe in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that at least six trees were blown down near Coosa.  The Rome News Tribune reported that many trees were blown down on several streets around Rome, including Faye Street, Dellwood Drive, Huffaker Road, Melrose Drive, Mount Alto Road, Horseleg Creek Road, and Hycliff Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damage to trees, power lines, and several structures across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grain silo was destroyed.  Several buildings were blown off their foundations.  Several trees were uprooted.  Sixty-two structures sustained damage to shingles and roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damage to trees, power lines, and several structures across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front was located across north Georgia.  A weak trough was moving from the Ohio Valley into the mid-Atlantic and southeast United States.  A strong Bermuda high was present off the southeast United States coast.  This was keeping a warm, moist, and unstable air mass over the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed across mainly central Georgia where moisture content was higher and the air mass was more unstable.  A few of these became severe, with hail being the primary element.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired meteorologist measured wind gusts of 55 to 60 mph just southwest of Warner Robins.  The Sun News of Warner Robins reported that the facade of the Town and County Motorsports was blown off by strong downburst winds and parts of the concrete wall of the Audio Shop were torn down.  Both of these businesses are along Carl Vinson Parkway in Warner Robins.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sun News of Warner Robins reported that lightning struck next to a Warner Robins police officer's patrol car while parked at the McConnell-Talbert Stadium parking lot during a severe thunderstorm.  The lightning left a hole in the asphalt parking lot next to the policeman's patrol car.  While no personal injury was suffered while the police officer was in the vehicle, the vehicle suffered damage to electronic circuitry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough continued to dig southward into the eastern United States.  A cold front also continued to move south toward Georgia. One thunderstorm in Macon county produced a strong downburst which resulted in at least 50 downed trees and dozens of power lines.  At least 10 homes were damaged by this isolated thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that one tree was down on a house and another tree was down on a vehicle.  Two people were trapped inside the vehicle, but no injuries were reported.  The Rome News Tribune reported that at least eight trees were down across the county.  In addition to the vehicle incident noted above, the porch of a home on Forsyth Street in Rome was damaged by a downed tree.  A power line was also brought down by this tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was deepening over the Ohio Valley.  High pressure aloft over the southeast United States was weakening and shifting west into the southern plains.  The atmosphere across north and central Georgia became very unstable during the afternoon as temperatures soared into the mid 90s with dewpoints in the low and mid 70s.  This combined with weak mesoscale boundaries set the stage for scattered to numerous thunderstorms to develop by mid-afternoon.  The hot temperatures allowed the activity to continue well into the evening and beyond sunset.  The activity initially began around the periphery of the upper high to the west in east Georgia then spread southward from Tennessee into north Georgia later and continued propagating southward during the evening hours.  Several downbursts were reported resulting in a number of downed trees and power lines, especially across north and east Georgia.  A few marginal severe hail events were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hancock County Emergency Management Director observed penny-sized hail in downtown Sparta at the court house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms across southwest Georgia on the 7th produced numerous reports of wind damage, as well as large hail and intense lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported just northeast of Delmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front was draped across north and central Georgia into central and northern Alabama.  Northwest flow continued aloft as a deep upper low remained over the northeast United States.  These features combined with ample heat and humidity allowed once again for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the heat of the day.  The activity remained below severe limits, but one thunderstorm in Upson county produced minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County Emergency Management Director reported that two power lines were down on the west side of Thomaston.  In addition, rainfall of 3.1 to 3.6 inches was reported in a one to two hour period resulting in minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low continued to deepen across the northeast U.S. and was digging southward into the southeast United States  A strong cold front was moving south from the Ohio Valley toward the southeast United States.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in Alabama during the early afternoon and expanded and spread eastward into western Georgia during the mid-afternoon.  Many of these storms produced damaging wind and large hail.  An expansive outflow boundary spread eastward across the state in advance of the thunderstorms causing additional thunderstorms to develop further eastward.  However, thunderstorms decreased in coverage as they spread further east and all of the activity had ended by 10 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning resulted in one structural fire as well as setting the adjacent field on fire.  A separate brush fire was also reported as a result of lightning.  In addition, smoke was reported in one home as a result of a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front remained draped across north Georgia with a warm, humid air mass firmly entrenched across the region south of the front as a large Bermuda high remained in place off the southeast United States coast.  Meanwhile, a weak upper trough was moving from the Midwest toward the lower Ohio valley.  Embedded within this trough were the remnants of Hurricane Dolly which had tracked from the lower Rio Grande Valley of Texas the Friday before, northwest into the Texas Panhandle, and then eastward across northern Oklahoma by this point in time.  The remnants of Dolly were being steered by a large subtropical upper ridge centered over North Texas.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening, several of which became severe producing damaging downburst winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator relayed a report of quarter-sized hail in the Banks Crossing area of southern Banks county.  This thunderstorm cell also impacted extreme northern Jackson county north of Commerce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front continued to approach Georgia from the northwest.  Weak short waves were embedded within a weak westerly flow aloft as a broad ridge of high pressure covered much of the southern U.S.  These combined with the usual warm, moist atmosphere in place in July to result in a continuation of scattered to numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms.  One particular area that was hard hit was Emanuel county in east central Georgia.  Several outflow boundaries from earlier afternoon thunderstorms merged over this area resulting in several hours of thunderstorms persisting over the same locations.  This resulted in localized flash flooding.  Marginal severe thunderstorms were observed elsewhere within the county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emanuel County 911 Center reported that rainfall of four to six inches across eastern Emanuel county from near Modoc to southeast of Swainsboro resulted in flooding of Hughes Creek, a tributary of Canoochee Creek, caused flooding of several roads in the area.  Canoochee Road was closed when at least a foot of swift moving water was flowing over the road.  Georgia Highway 56, just north of Swainsboro, was also closed when at least eight inches of water was flowing over the road.  Other roads were flooded in the area from lesser amounts of standing and/or flowing water, including Lambs Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was moving southward into North Georgia while an upper-level short wave was passing over the area in west-northwest flow aloft.  Surface conditions were very warm and moist.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed around noon and continued into the early evening.  While no severe thunderstorms were reported, frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Herald of McDonough reported that several lightning-related structural fires were set in the McDonough area.  However, no major damage was reported from any of the incidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain generated flooding on SR 125 just south of Waterloo in Irwin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from torrential rain caused the Hat Creek to overflow its banks, which flooded State Road 125, just south of the town of Waterloo.  The Irwin County DOT closed the road for an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms on the 9th caused scattered reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to golf ball size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper-level trough and associated cold front were approaching the area from the west.  This triggered scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 28th.  Several of these became severe.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also an issue.  The system became even stronger on the 29th producing widespread severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia determined that strong straight-line wind of 70 to 80 mph affected a small area of southern Whitfield county, approximately three miles southwest of Dalton.  Damage was concentrated along a 100 yard long and 250 yard wide path along Brookview Lane.  Several trees were snapped and hundreds of large limbs were ripped from trees.  Some power lines were down in the area as well.  Several fences were damaged from the downed trees and tree limbs and minor to moderate roof damage was observed to three homes and one outbuilding in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low continued to deepen across the northeast U.S. and was digging southward into the southeast United States  A strong cold front was moving south from the Ohio Valley toward the southeast United States.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in Alabama during the early afternoon and expanded and spread eastward into western Georgia during the mid-afternoon.  Many of these storms produced damaging wind and large hail.  An expansive outflow boundary spread eastward across the state in advance of the thunderstorms causing additional thunderstorms to develop further eastward.  However, thunderstorms decreased in coverage as they spread further east and all of the activity had ended by 10 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to nickel-sized hail in the northern portion of the county northwest of Cumming and in the Silver City area.  Radar data supports that hail of this size or larger likely occurred across a large portion of north central Forsyth county.

Penny-sized hail was also reported just northwest of Lawrenceville, but this appeared to have been a different thunderstorm cell.  In addition, several reports of three to over four inches of rain were received from the Ducula area with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough was sweeping from the Ohio Valley into the southeast U.S.  An unusually strong mid-summer cold front accompanied the upper trough.  These features combined with a moist tropical air mass present over the region resulted in the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the heat of the afternoon and early evening.  Many of these thunderstorms became severe and damaging straight-line and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that at least a dozen trees were down just south of downtown Atlanta.  Two homes on Adella Street were damaged a a result of trees that fell on the structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 24th tropical storm Fay reached its western most position in extreme southeastern Louisiana.  By the 25th, Fay had resumed a northeastward motion around a weakness in the upper ridge and in advance of an approaching frontal system.  After heating combined with the abundant tropical moisture over the area during the afternoon of the 24th to produce scattered to numerous bands of showers and thunderstorms, many with locally very heavy rain and frequency cloud-to-ground lightning.  However, no flash flooding or severe weather was reported on the 24th.  By the 25th, the remnants of Fay had moved northeast back into Mississippi.  Outer rain bands began to spread back into west Georgia by morning and continued moving east during the day.  With daytime heating, these bands of showers and thunderstorms intensified.  Several thunderstorm cells exhibited rotation on Doppler radar during the afternoon and evening and prompted tornado warnings.  There was one report of a funnel cloud in Upson county, but no confirmed tornado touch downs were observed and no damage was reported on the 25th.||Locally heavy rain was also an issue, especially in the northeast Georgia mountain counties where heavy rain had persisted during the morning hours.  Significant rises were observed on creeks, streams, and rivers in the area, but none exceeded their banks in the Peachtree City warning forecast area and no flooding/flash flooding was reported on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed a funnel cloud just north-northeast of Thomaston.  This funnel cloud was being observed from the same thunderstorm cell that spawned funnel clouds in southwest Upson county 20 minutes earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front lingered across northeast Georgia.  High pressure aloft continued to build from the Arklatex region into the mid-south and southeast U.S.  A strong upper trough was moving off the New England Coast, with a weak extension of the trough trailing into southeast Georgia.  This feature combined with ample daytime heating and moisture provided for afternoon thunderstorms across east central Georgia.  The activity mainly affected Emanuel, Johnson, and Washington counties.  Isolated storms became severe, but all produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An officer with the Wadley Police Department observed penny-sized hail in the Wadley area.

The intermittent damage path was mainly limited to downed trees and power lines, although some minor structural damage was noted near Reed Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County 911 Center reported that the roof of an apartment building was damaged by strong winds in a thunderstorm associated with the outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay.  A portion of the roof collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper trough was sweeping through the Great Lakes and Northeast from the 2nd into the 3rd of August.  This was pushing an equally unusually strong cold front for early August into the southeast U.S.  This front combined with a very warm humid air mass, partially a result of the lingering remnants of Hurricane Dolly, to result in the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms in far north Georgia during the mid to late afternoon hours.  Thunderstorms across north Georgia merged into a near solid arched band of thunderstorms which raced southward during the evening hours into the Atlanta metropolitan area.  Large hail and intense cloud-to-ground lightning accompanied the thunderstorms in north Georgia.  Leading outflow winds produced a wind gust of 71 mph at Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport as the thunderstorms came through around 730 pm EDT.  The thunderstorms subsequently diminished as they pushed further south of the Atlanta area, but redeveloped once again into strong to severe thunderstorms during the late evening as the outflow boundary pushed into the Macon area and nearby counties.  Numerous reports of downed trees were received from many north and central Georgia counties as a result of these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon County 911 Center reported that several trees were blown down in the Garden Valley area.  Radar data showed a significant downburst occurred in this region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions persisted and even expanded slightly westward to cover more of far western North Carolina and northeast Georgia during the month. During the early part of the month, flows on most of the major streams across the area were running at record low levels. Only a handful of streams were running at more than 1 to 7 percent of normal. Groundwater levels were 2-5 feet below normal. Significant agricultural impacts persisted, with losses to summer crops, including hay, estimated at 30 percent. The dry weather also affected the livestock industry, due to shortages of pasture crops necessary for feeding. ||By the end of the month, tropical storm Fay had dropped several inches of rainfall across the area, providing some relief from the drought conditions. CoCoRaHS observers in parts of northeast Georgia reported over 10 inches of rain from the remnants of Tropical Storm Fay.
A severe storm produced large hail over northeast Georgia during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported near the intersection of Hillshore Rd and Lake View Dr.

Trees were reported down along Lanier Road, South Apple Valley Road, Georgia Highway 60, Georgia Highway 335, Kingsbridge Road, and near the Jefferson First Baptist Church.  One tree fell on a home in the Commerce area causing moderate damage to the structure.  In addition, damage was also reported to the porch of a home on Harp Street in the Commerce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Georgetown restaurant was destroyed by fire as a direct result of a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Georgetown restaurant was destroyed by fire.  The cause of the blaze was lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm uprooted trees and damaged a storage building and barn just east of Jakin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm uprooted several pecan trees just east of Jakin.  A nearby storage building and barn were damaged.  Several trees fell across King-Santi Road.  A large tree fell over a pasture fence where  several of the cows escaped and wandered onto U.S. Highway 84.  Three vehicles traveling in the eastbound lane struck four cows.  Two drivers and three passengers were injured and transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was located across north Georgia.  A strong subtropical ridge continued to build to the west over the southern plains and was beginning to spread east into the Mid-South and Southeast.  A deep upper trough was moving through New England.  While the atmosphere was less favorable for thunderstorm development than had been observed the previous day, isolated thunderstorms still developed during the heat of the afternoon south of the frontal boundary.  Most of the activity was south of an Atlanta to Milledgeville line.  While no severe events were reported, frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was common with all of the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported that two homes were struck and set on fire by lightning.  One of the homes was on Georgia Highway 15 and the other was on Archer Pond Road off Georgia Highway 15, just northeast of Scott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second day of significant effects from tropical storm Fay on the north and central Georgia area as the storm drifted slowly west across the Florida panhandle into far southern Alabama.  The outer rain bands brought gusty winds and heavy rain to the far southern and southwestern part of the forecast area.  The strong winds and rain resulted in several trees and some power lines being blown down in areas from Columbus, to Americus and Cordele.  Rainfall of three to four inches fell in the southwest counties from Stewart to Crisp, with two to three inch rainfall amounts further north toward Muscogee, Marion, and Schley counties.  Since the area overall was in a continuing drought condition, no flooding was reported within the Peachtree City forecast area.||Here are some of the specific wind damage reports that were received from the southwestern counties, all a result of winds associated with the outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay.||The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that a few trees were blown down in the Columbus area.  A few of these landed on power lines.  About 250 people were left without power.||The Crisp County Emergency Management Director reported that wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph for much of the previous 24 hour period combined with persistent moderate rain had resulted in up to 15 trees being blown down across the county.||The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that persistent strong and gusty winds combined with a steady moderate to heavy rain had resulted in up to 30 trees being blown down across the county during the previous 24 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that a few trees were blown down in the Columbus area.  A few of these landed on power lines.  About 250 people were left without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary lingered across north Georgia.  A strong subtropical high pressure system remained over the southern plains, with an upper trough aligned just off the east coast.  The atmosphere over the region was largely more stable than observed during previous days.  However, strong daytime heating combined with ample surface moisture to allow at least isolated, small thunderstorms to develop by late afternoon and early evening.  The thunderstorms dissipated almost as quickly as they developed, generally within a 30 minute period.  As the thunderstorms dissipated, they produced strong downdraft winds.  One of these small thunderstorms produced a significant downburst near Lake Allatoona in far southeast Bartow county downing more than 100 trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst with estimated winds in excess of 65 mph from a small collapsing thunderstorms downed over 100 trees in far southeast Bartow county around Lake Allatoona.  At least 12 of the downed trees were large oak trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was located along the east coast of the U.S. and trailed southwestward into central Georgia.  A surface trough was located across central and south Georgia.  The remnants of Hurricane Dolly had been pushed south into central and south Georgia ahead of the trough.  Ample moisture combined with active northwest flow aloft and the surface boundary to allow scattered strong thunderstorms to develop during the late afternoon and evening hours across central and south Georgia.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed in these storms, but none reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wheeler County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down on a power line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County 911 Center reported that more than a dozen trees were blown down across the county.  Doppler radar base velocity data indicated wind speeds up to 65 mph tracked across much of the county from northwest to southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper trough was sweeping through the Great Lakes and Northeast from the 2nd into the 3rd of August.  This was pushing an equally unusually strong cold front for early August into the southeast U.S.  This front combined with a very warm humid air mass, partially a result of the lingering remnants of Hurricane Dolly, to result in the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms in far north Georgia during the mid to late afternoon hours.  Thunderstorms across north Georgia merged into a near solid arched band of thunderstorms which raced southward during the evening hours into the Atlanta metropolitan area.  Large hail and intense cloud-to-ground lightning accompanied the thunderstorms in north Georgia.  Leading outflow winds produced a wind gust of 71 mph at Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport as the thunderstorms came through around 730 pm EDT.  The thunderstorms subsequently diminished as they pushed further south of the Atlanta area, but redeveloped once again into strong to severe thunderstorms during the late evening as the outflow boundary pushed into the Macon area and nearby counties.  Numerous reports of downed trees were received from many north and central Georgia counties as a result of these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed nickel-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were down across the county from two miles southwest of Canton, to seven miles southeast of Canton to Ballground in the northern part of the county.  Power lines were brought down with the trees in several instances and several roads were blocked as a result.  Wind gusts of 49 mph were measured at the Canton Emergency Operations Center at 845 am EDT.  All together at least six trees and at least four power lines were downed.  A home was damaged two miles southwest of Canton when a large tree fell onto the structure.  A large tree was down on power lines in downtown Ballground blocking the road.  A large tree was also down on power lines seven miles southeast of Canton blocking a road.  Trees were also down on power lines five miles southeast of Canton.  Two large trees were down on a power line five miles north of Canton, also blocking the road.  Finally, a large tree was down four miles northwest of Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions persisted and even expanded slightly westward to cover more of far western North Carolina and northeast Georgia during the month. During the early part of the month, flows on most of the major streams across the area were running at record low levels. Only a handful of streams were running at more than 1 to 7 percent of normal. Groundwater levels were 2-5 feet below normal. Significant agricultural impacts persisted, with losses to summer crops, including hay, estimated at 30 percent. The dry weather also affected the livestock industry, due to shortages of pasture crops necessary for feeding. ||By the end of the month, tropical storm Fay had dropped several inches of rainfall across the area, providing some relief from the drought conditions. CoCoRaHS observers in parts of northeast Georgia reported over 10 inches of rain from the remnants of Tropical Storm Fay.

This was pushing an equally unusually strong cold front for early August into the southeast U.S.  This front combined with a very warm humid air mass, partially a result of the lingering remnants of Hurricane Dolly, to result in the development of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms in far north Georgia during the mid to late afternoon hours.  Thunderstorms across north Georgia merged into a near solid arched band of thunderstorms which raced southward during the evening hours into the Atlanta metropolitan area.  Large hail and intense cloud-to-ground lightning accompanied the thunderstorms in north Georgia.  Leading outflow winds produced a wind gust of 71 mph at Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport as the thunderstorms came through around 730 pm EDT.  The thunderstorms subsequently diminished as they pushed further south of the Atlanta area, but redeveloped once again into strong to severe thunderstorms during the late evening as the outflow boundary pushed into the Macon area and nearby counties.  Numerous reports of downed trees were received from many north and central Georgia counties as a result of these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail in the southwest corner of Gilmer county near Carter's Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions persisted and even expanded slightly westward to cover more of far western North Carolina and northeast Georgia during the month. During the early part of the month, flows on most of the major streams across the area were running at record low levels. Only a handful of streams were running at more than 1 to 7 percent of normal. Groundwater levels were 2-5 feet below normal. Significant agricultural impacts persisted, with losses to summer crops, including hay, estimated at 30 percent. The dry weather also affected the livestock industry, due to shortages of pasture crops necessary for feeding. ||By the end of the month, tropical storm Fay had dropped several inches of rainfall across the area, providing some relief from the drought conditions. CoCoRaHS observers in parts of northeast Georgia reported over 10 inches of rain from the remnants of Tropical Storm Fay.

A deepening upper level trough over the southeast region lifted a deep plume of tropical moisture over the forecast area. A strong low level 850 mb jet of 50-55 knots was in place. Backing winds advected instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico across North Florida which coincided with the passing of a second pre-frontal squall line as it tracked across North Florida and Southeast Georgia in the afternoon. Low level storm relative helicities increased to 200-350 M2/S2. Widespread severe storms developed across the forecast area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EMA reported 22 large trees were blown down throughout the county. The downed trees landed on four homes, two businesses and four trailer homes. The event lasted about 12 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about three miles north of Enterprise.  It first uprooted trees and tore the roof off a home on Mayhaw Road, then traveled norrheast where it damaged a barn and destroyed a irrigation pivot on Joe Shingler Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just north of Lenox near U.S. Highway 41 and Darden Road.  It traveled northwest and passed over Interstate 75.  Along its path, it downed numerous trees and heavily damaged a barn near Sugar Hill Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that thunderstorm downburst winds blew down dozens of trees on the east and north side of Lake Allatoona. Up to 25,000 people in the area were left without power for several hours following the storm.  The Cherokee Tribune reported that up to 134 homes in the area sustained at least minor damage from wind and/or lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough was sweeping from the Ohio Valley into the southeast U.S.  An unusually strong mid-summer cold front accompanied the upper trough.  These features combined with a moist tropical air mass present over the region resulted in the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the heat of the afternoon and early evening.  Many of these thunderstorms became severe and damaging straight-line and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail south of Rayle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front and upper-level trough continued to approach Georgia from the northwest as strong low pressure remained over the northeast United States.  This combined with ample daytime heating and moisture produced scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  Several of the storms became severe and produced penny to quarter-sized hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in far western Newton county on Georgia Highway 212 near the Rockdale county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms across southwest Georgia on the 7th produced numerous reports of wind damage, as well as large hail and intense lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and a utility pole were down near County Road 374 and Joel Pool Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the upper low to the northeast of Georgia, across the mid-Atlantic was weakening, a weak upper trough lingered across this region.  This combined with typical July heat and humidity was more than adequate for another afternoon of significant pulse thunderstorms, several of which became severe.  The activity lingered into the early morning hours of the 9th.  There was even one fatality in Banks county in northeast Georgia when a tree fell on a house.  The slow movement and clustering of thunderstorms across north central Georgia in Forsyth, Dawson, and Lumpkin counties prompted flash flood warnings.  A minor flash flood event was observed in Dawson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning strikes at several locations caused electrical transformers to blow and resulted in damage to audio equipment at several homes.  However, no structural fires were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms across southwest Georgia on the 7th produced numerous reports of wind damage, as well as large hail and intense lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A transformer was down on Three Notch Road just north of Joel Pool Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Banks County Emergency Management Director reported that more than a dozen trees were down across the county.  A number of trees were down in the western part of the county along Georgia Highway 51 and Hickory Flat Road between Gillsville and Homer, and in the eastern part of the county on Georgia Highway 51 between U.S. Highway 441 and the Franklin county line.  A 70 year-old male was killed on Rock Springs Road, approximately four miles northwest of Homer, when a tree fell on his home.  He was killed instantly when the tree fell on the bedroom portion of his mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough was amplifying and sweeping through the eastern and southeastern United States.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper trough.  While instability was minimal and no significant thunderstorms were observed with this system in north or central Georgia, a gravity wave developed on the back edge of a large area of light to moderate rain as it was moving into the eastern portions of central and south Georgia.  This gravity wave resulted in strong to high winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph, causing a number of trees to be blown down in several counties between Macon and Vidalia.  Wind gusts likely reached or exceeded 60 mph in Bibb county, which experienced the most extensive damage during this event.||The following is a summary of events from the affected counties.  The Macon Telegraph reported that very strong winds blew down numerous trees and dozens of power lines throughout the county.  Windows were blown out of restaurant in downtown Macon and approximately 10,000 residents, mostly in the Bloomfield and north Macon area, were left without power for several hours following the event.  There were also several reports of garage doors being blown in.  Doppler radar base velocity data showed 50-60 knot winds affecting much of Bibb county on the back side of a rain area moving eastward through the county during this time frame.  Wind damage was also reported from Baldwin, Johnson, Laurens, Montgomery, Toombs, Toombs, and Wilkinson counties.  Of these counties, the most significant damage was in Baldwin county, where several trees fell on power lines leaving approximately 6000 residents in the area from Milledgeville to Meriwether without power for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs Count 911 Center reported that at least three trees were blown down across Toombs and Montgomery counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved over Southeast Georgia allowing scattered strong to severe convection to form in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that several trees and power lines were blown down in near Berryville, Georgia causing Union Springs Road to be blocked near Stillwell Clyo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure over the northern Gulf of Mexico along with a warm front off the South Carolina coast created strong gradient winds and uplift within a moist environment that led to heavy convective rainfall. The timing of the event coincided with an evening high tide along the Georgia coast, which aided in additional flooding problems near the Savannah, Georgia Harbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a road closure due to standing water at the intersection of Maythrop and Augusta Street in downtown Savannah, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front continued to march eastward through the state along with an upper low that was closing off over the southeast United States.  A weak stationary front and low pressure area remained stationary across far south Georgia along the Florida border.  Thunderstorms developed across south Georgia during the mid-morning hours and spread northward into the southern portions of central Georgia by late morning.  The storms took on the nature of isolated supercell thunderstorms and produced large hail and damaging winds.  The severe thunderstorms were short-lived and dissipated quickly after the noon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deputy with the Wilcox County Sheriff's Department observed baseball-sized hail in the Rochelle area.  The public observed golf ball-sized hail in the far northern part of the county in the Pineview area.  Several car windshields were observed to have been damaged in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported several trees down and no power on Arkola Road 4 miles east southeast of Brooklet, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough was amplifying and sweeping through the eastern and southeastern United States.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper trough.  While instability was minimal and no significant thunderstorms were observed with this system in north or central Georgia, a gravity wave developed on the back edge of a large area of light to moderate rain as it was moving into the eastern portions of central and south Georgia.  This gravity wave resulted in strong to high winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph, causing a number of trees to be blown down in several counties between Macon and Vidalia.  Wind gusts likely reached or exceeded 60 mph in Bibb county, which experienced the most extensive damage during this event.||The following is a summary of events from the affected counties.  The Macon Telegraph reported that very strong winds blew down numerous trees and dozens of power lines throughout the county.  Windows were blown out of restaurant in downtown Macon and approximately 10,000 residents, mostly in the Bloomfield and north Macon area, were left without power for several hours following the event.  There were also several reports of garage doors being blown in.  Doppler radar base velocity data showed 50-60 knot winds affecting much of Bibb county on the back side of a rain area moving eastward through the county during this time frame.  Wind damage was also reported from Baldwin, Johnson, Laurens, Montgomery, Toombs, Toombs, and Wilkinson counties.  Of these counties, the most significant damage was in Baldwin county, where several trees fell on power lines leaving approximately 6000 residents in the area from Milledgeville to Meriwether without power for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County 911 Center reported that at least three trees were blown down near the Toombs and Montgomery county lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in advance of a cold front and tracked through the southeast United States, resulting in several severe thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in three locations in Sylvania, Georgia between Main Street and Highway 21. One tree was reported down on a car at the local Mini Mart and another was down on a power line and caused a fire at a local business office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in advance of a cold front and tracked through the southeast United States, resulting in several severe thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported ten inch diameter tree limbs down in Garden City, Georgia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County 911 Center reported that less than 10 trees were blown down, scattered about the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure over the northern Gulf of Mexico along with a warm front off the South Carolina coast created strong gradient winds and uplift within a moist environment that led to heavy convective rainfall. The timing of the event coincided with an evening high tide along the Georgia coast, which aided in additional flooding problems near the Savannah, Georgia Harbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that several powerlines were down near the intersection of Colt Road and Garrard Road 5 miles west southwest of Savannah, Georgia near the Hunter Army Airfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported that several powerlines were down near the intersection of Habersham Street and 37th Street in downtown Savannah, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over southern Georgia along the cold front during the afternoon of the 8th, and with the trailing upper level low during the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds estimated between 60 and 70 mph damaged two vacant homes near Seminole State Park.  Several trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved over Southeast Georgia allowing scattered strong to severe convection to form in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported several trees and power lines blown down causing road closures 3 miles north northwest of Bloomingdale, Georgia. Minor damage to structures in the area was also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon Telegraph reported that very strong winds blew down numerous trees and dozens of power lines throughout the county.  At least 10,000 people in the Bloomfield and north Macon area left without power for several hours following the incident.  In addition, a large window was shattered at the Acapulco Mexican Restaurant in downtown Macon was shattered by the high winds.  At least seven other buildings in the downtown area sustained window damage from the high winds.  There were also several reports of garage doors being blown in by the strong winds.  Doppler radar base velocity data showed 50-60 knot winds affecting much of Bibb county on the back side of a rain area moving eastward through the county during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lumpkin County Emergency Management Director confirmed that flash flooding had occurred at several locations in eastern Lumpkin county as a result of very heavy rainfall caused by the remnants of tropical storm Fay.  Five-day total rainfall for the eastern portion of the county was in the six to eight inch range, but three to four inches of this fell on this day alone, resulting in flash flooding.  A mudslide was observed on Corporate Road upstream from the Chestatee River Bridge approximately 5.4 miles east-northeast of Dahlonega.  This resulted in the temporary closure of Corporate Road in this area.  The mudslide was 20 feet by 60 feet tall.  A culvert pipe on Wahoo Creek Road, a gravel road was washed out by flood waters.  This was located approximately 7.9 miles east of Dahlonega.  Finally, a stream crossing under Mount Olive Church Road, approximately 6.4 miles southeast of Dahlonega, overflowed its banks by at least a foot resulting in minor flooding of upstream agricultural fields.  Approximately six miles east of Dahlonega, a stream overflowed onto Georgia Highway 115 near Grindale Brothers Road by at least one foot.  Finally, Pecks Mill Creek overflowed its banks at the Pecks Mill Creek Road Bridge with at least two feet of water over the road.  Significant erosion occurred and the culvert pipe was damaged.  With the exception of this later incident, overall damage was minor and mostly in the form of debris cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved east across north Florida over night on Wednesday 12/10 and during the day on Thursday 12/11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down near 1421 College Avenue destroying a vinyl storage shed. A more substantial wood shed next to it was undamaged and no significant damage to vegetation was observed in this vicinity.  In this area the event was a low level EF-0 event with winds estimated near 65 mph. The system next moved over Burney Branch Lake which allowed the circulation to intensify with winds now closer to an upper end EF-0 and estimated to be near 85 mph.  The tornado made landfall near 1101 Lakeview Drive where several trees were snapped. The funnel moved over several lakeside homes and their backyards in this area. A vinyl fence was blown down and a wooden child���s backyard play set was uprooted and overturned. At the northeastern edge of the lake the most substantial damage occurred when the roof of a dock was ripped off and blown onto the roof of a home at 919 Edgewood Circle.  The home���s roof was destroyed opening the contents to the weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front continued to march eastward through the state along with an upper low that was closing off over the southeast United States.  A weak stationary front and low pressure area remained stationary across far south Georgia along the Florida border.  Thunderstorms developed across south Georgia during the mid-morning hours and spread northward into the southern portions of central Georgia by late morning.  The storms took on the nature of isolated supercell thunderstorms and produced large hail and damaging winds.  The severe thunderstorms were short-lived and dissipated quickly after the noon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilcox County Emergency Management Director reported that thunderstorm winds resulted in damage to several chicken houses.  The roof was blown off nine chicken homes and five others were left leaning slightly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and associated upper trough were moving through the mid south into the southeast during the afternoon of the 8th.  A wedge front was located across extreme southern Georgia and northern Florida, with a relatively stable air mass over most of the region.  Bands of heavy rain showers developed along and ahead of the front in west Georgia during the early afternoon and began spreading rapidly eastward. While instability was minimal, thanks to the wedge front, strong shear associated with the upper system allowed one brief EF0 tornado to develop in north Fayette county causing considerable damage to a large shopping mall area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a very brief EF0 tornado had touched down in the northeast part of the Fayette Pavilion Shopping center, just north of downtown Fayetteville located between Georgia Highways 314 and 85.  The tornado caused the back wall of the two-story Christian Bookstore to buckle outward.  An air conditioning unit was also blown off the roof pad of the adjacent Marshalls Department store.  A few trees behind these buildings were also blown down.  Minor damage was also observed to the facade and utility lines of adjacent buildings.  The tornado path length was 150 yards long with a maximum path width of 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front continued to march eastward through the state along with an upper low that was closing off over the southeast United States.  A weak stationary front and low pressure area remained stationary across far south Georgia along the Florida border.  Thunderstorms developed across south Georgia during the mid-morning hours and spread northward into the southern portions of central Georgia by late morning.  The storms took on the nature of isolated supercell thunderstorms and produced large hail and damaging winds.  The severe thunderstorms were short-lived and dissipated quickly after the noon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pulaski County Emergency Management Director reported quarter-sized hail north of Pineview and nickel-sized hail was reported in Finleyson.  Golf ball-sized hail was also reported in Wilcox county near the Pulaski county line.  Radar data supports hail of quarter or larger size from the Wilcox county line just south of Finleyson to just north of Wallace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front and very deep upper trough were sweeping from the central into the eastern U.S. from the 7th into the 8th.  A surface low pressure area was developing in response to this synoptic pattern along the southeast U.S. coast.  Widespread showers and thunderstorms accompanied the system through Georgia during the morning hours, moving out of the region during the early afternoon.  However, additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening hours in advance of the strong cold front.  As this occurred, a couple of these thunderstorms, albeit small, became severe.  One spawned an EF1 tornado in the Chapel Hill area of Douglas county, causing significant damage to a large grocery store and at least 50 homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia concluded that a tornado, with a maximum intensity of EF1, tracked nearly seven miles across eastern Douglas county.  Maximum path width was determined to be 100 yards and maximum wind speeds were estimated at 110 mph, near the upper end of the EF1 scale.  The tornado, which began as an EF0 tornado, touched down in the 4300 block of Hampton Mill Drive in Chapel Hill, or about 4 miles south-southeast of Douglasville.  Damage at this location was confined to minor roof damage and several snapped trees.  The tornado continued across Stratford Drive as an EF0 where several more trees were snapped or bent over and a fence was blown down.  The tornado then strengthened to an EF1 tornado as it crossed into a shopping center at the intersection of Bomar and Chapel Hill Roads.  A Kroger grocery store sustained considerable damage when an air conditioning unit was blown off the roof, causing considerable damage to the roof of the building.  An employee of the Kroger store was injured with a broken arm during the tornado. The tornado then continued as an EF1 into the Ashland Subdivision where numerous homes sustained minor roof damage and many trees were either uprooted or snapped.  A number of homes in this area also sustained broken windows.  A portion of the Kroger grocery store roof was found scattered about this neighborhood.  As the EF1 continued on its north-northeastward track from this area, a mobile home was destroyed when a large pine tree fell on the structure.  A number of large trees were also uprooted in the area.  A trampoline was found up in the trees.  The tornado than continued toward Ossabaw Court and Brookhollow, where winds associated with the tornado were estimated to have reached their maximum intensity of 110 mph.  Numerous trees were blown down, the north side of a home was destroyed, a garage was damaged, a chimney blown down, and several homes sustained roof damage.  As the tornado continued toward Warren and Pope Road, a shed was destroyed, several more large trees were uprooted, and shingles were blown off the roofs of several homes.  Debris throughout the area was scattered up to 200 yards from its origin.  The tornado continued on its north-northeastward track  and began to weaken back to an EF0 as it tracked across Georgia Highway 92 and into the southern portions of the Lithia Springs area.  A few large trees were blown down along Georgia Highway 92 just west of Bomar Road.  In the southern part of Lithia Springs, several homes on Annette Drive suffered minor roof damage, a fence was blown down, a basketball goal blown over, and several trees were uprooted.  The tornado ended near the intersection of Beaver Run Creek Road and Vulcan Drive, where several trees were down.||All together, one mobile home and one site-built home were destroyed, 10 homes sustained major damage, 52 sustained minor damage, 1 business had major damage, and 1 business had minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough was sweeping through the mid U.S. and into the eastern U.S.  A wedge of colder air was noted across north Georgia.  These two weather systems combined to bring widespread rain and gusty winds to much of north and central Georgia during the early morning hours of the 24th.  Strong winds resulted in several tree limbs falling on power lines in the Rockmart area of Polk county.  This left about 300 residents in the area without power for several hours during the early morning.  While strong winds existed in other areas, this was the only report of damage received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockmark Journal reported that strong winds and rain resulted in power outages to approximately 300 residents in the area when tree limbs fell on power lines.  Most of the outages were in the Goodyear Village neighborhood along Third Avenue.

Of these counties, the most significant damage was in Baldwin county, where several trees fell on power lines leaving approximately 6000 residents in the area from Milledgeville to Meriwether without power for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County 911 Center reported that two trees and one power line were blown down in the southern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved over Southeast Georgia allowing scattered strong to severe convection to form in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fireman reported penny to quarter size hail lasting about 5 minutes 1 mile northwest of Darien, Georgia at exit 49 along interstate 95.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: WMAZ Television of Macon relayed a report of a funnel cloud from the public just on Georgia Highway 22 just east of Gray.  Doppler radar supported rotation in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay will be remembered from the catastrophic rainfall that it brought to much of Florida as well as being one of the longer lived tropical systems to affect the U.S.  Tropical Storm Fay formed from a tropical wave on August 15th along the east coast of Hispaniola.  The storm moved west along the south coast of Cuba before reaching a weakness in the subtropical ridge to its north causing it turn north-northwestward into central Cuba by the 17th.  Fay continued to track north-northwest through the Florida straits and Florida Keys on the 18th before turning northeast toward the Florida peninsula.  The storm made landfall on August 19th in southwest Florida at Cape Romano as a tropical storm with winds of 60 mph.  Fay continued to move very slowly northeast across the central Florida peninsula, well maintaining its strength along the way, reaching the northeast coast of Florida on August 21st.  At this point, a strengthening subtropical ridge and surface high over the mid-Atlantic region forced Fay to take a sharp westward turn toward the Florida panhandle.  The storm tracked west to west-northwest from the 22nd through the 25th into extreme southeast Louisiana before reaching the western end of the subtropical ridge and an approaching frontal system.  Thus, Fay once again turned back toward the northeast across central Mississippi and central/northern Alabama before finally becoming absorbed into the mean flow and a frontal system located across the Tennessee Valley.||The slow movement of Fay and the proximity to the forecast area allowed for Fay's impacts on the Peachtree City forecast area to last several days.  Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

Fay continued to move very slowly northeast across the central Florida peninsula, well maintaining its strength along the way, reaching the northeast coast of Florida on August 21st.  At this point, a strengthening subtropical ridge and surface high over the mid-Atlantic region forced Fay to take a sharp westward turn toward the Florida panhandle.  The storm tracked west to west-northwest from the 22nd through the 25th into extreme southeast Louisiana before reaching the western end of the subtropical ridge and an approaching frontal system.  Thus, Fay once again turned back toward the northeast across central Mississippi and central/northern Alabama before finally becoming absorbed into the mean flow and a frontal system located across the Tennessee Valley.||The slow movement of Fay and the proximity to the forecast area allowed for Fay's impacts on the Peachtree City forecast area to last several days.  Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Emergency Management Director relayed a report of penny-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia and information obtained from the White County Emergency Management Director confirmed that flash flooding had occurred at several locations in White County as a result of heavy rain associated with the remnants of tropical storm Fay.  Cumulative rainfall of six to eight inches fell across much of White county during the 5-day period that Fay affected the area.  However, in excess of three inches of this rainfall occurred on the 26th.  Flash flooding was observed along the Chattahoochee River at Helen when the river reached the flood stage of six feet at 230 pm EDT.  Minor flooding of parks and tubing businesses along the river was observed.  In addition, Brucken Strasse near the Ramada Inn was temporarily closed because of flooding and a sidewalk along Troll Tavern was also closed.  Approximately five miles west-northwest of Cleveland, a culvert pipe and asphalt road were washed out at 277 Mill Lane from flash flooding.  Finally, approximately four miles northwest of Cleveland, Towns Creek rose out of its banks and flooded several yards in portions of the Paradise Valley Campground.  Towns Creek went into flood at Adair Mill Road overflowing the culvert with at least one foot of water flowing over the road.  Finally, there was a mudslide on Kellum Valley Road approximately five miles northwest of Cleveland causing temporary closure of the road.  The mudslide was 15 feet wide by 20 feet tall.  With the exception to damage to Mill Lane, damage from these incidents was minor and mainly in the form of debris cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a nearly week long lull from the daily summer convective activity, scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed across the area again.  A weak front, that drifted into the state during the previous weekend, had stalled about halfway across the state.  The front combined with moisture from the extreme outer circulation of hurricane Ike, located near Cuba, and potentially left over residual moisture that drifted over the area from the remnants of hurricane Gustav, provided a favorable environment for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the storms became marginally severe and produced damaging winds.  One storm produced large hail.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Murray County 911 Center reported that two trees were blown down in the southern part of the county and two more trees were blown down in the northern part of the county.  Doppler base velocity data showed that the strongest winds were just west of Fort Mountain State Park.

Approximately six miles north to northeast of Rome, trees were down across Sam Harris, Bells Ferry, Bert, and Old Dalton Road.  In Shannon, a tree was down across a power line on 2nd Street.

Fay continued to track north-northwest through the Florida straits and Florida Keys on the 18th before turning northeast toward the Florida peninsula.  The storm made landfall on August 19th in southwest Florida at Cape Romano as a tropical storm with winds of 60 mph. The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that six trees were down in western Fulton county, west and northwest of East Point, near the intersection of Butner Road and Campbellton Road, including Howell Mill Road and Acorn Avenue.  A little later and a little further north, two more trees were blown down near the intersection of Mount Paran Road and Northside Drive.
