Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

After a nearly week long lull from the daily summer convective activity, scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed across the area again.  A weak front, that drifted into the state during the previous weekend, had stalled about halfway across the state.  The front combined with moisture from the extreme outer circulation of hurricane Ike, located near Cuba, and potentially left over residual moisture that drifted over the area from the remnants of hurricane Gustav, provided a favorable environment for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the storms became marginally severe and produced damaging winds.  One storm produced large hail.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paulding County 911 Center reported that four trees were down on power lines in the northwest part of the county.

Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center reported that three to four trees were down on Colonial Club Drive in the Euharlee area.  A storm spotter reported that a large oak tree was down on Mission Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a nearly week long lull from the daily summer convective activity, scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed across the area again.  A weak front, that drifted into the state during the previous weekend, had stalled about halfway across the state.  The front combined with moisture from the extreme outer circulation of hurricane Ike, located near Cuba, and potentially left over residual moisture that drifted over the area from the remnants of hurricane Gustav, provided a favorable environment for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the storms became marginally severe and produced damaging winds.  One storm produced large hail.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County 911 Center reported that lightning struck a home on Colonial Club Drive and started a fire.  Specific damages were not provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay will be remembered from the catastrophic rainfall that it brought to much of Florida as well as being one of the longer lived tropical systems to affect the U.S.  Tropical Storm Fay formed from a tropical wave on August 15th along the east coast of Hispaniola.  The storm moved west along the south coast of Cuba before reaching a weakness in the subtropical ridge to its north causing it turn north-northwestward into central Cuba by the 17th.  Fay continued to track north-northwest through the Florida straits and Florida Keys on the 18th before turning northeast toward the Florida peninsula.  The storm made landfall on August 19th in southwest Florida at Cape Romano as a tropical storm with winds of 60 mph.  Fay continued to move very slowly northeast across the central Florida peninsula, well maintaining its strength along the way, reaching the northeast coast of Florida on August 21st.  At this point, a strengthening subtropical ridge and surface high over the mid-Atlantic region forced Fay to take a sharp westward turn toward the Florida panhandle.  The storm tracked west to west-northwest from the 22nd through the 25th into extreme southeast Louisiana before reaching the western end of the subtropical ridge and an approaching frontal system.  Thus, Fay once again turned back toward the northeast across central Mississippi and central/northern Alabama before finally becoming absorbed into the mean flow and a frontal system located across the Tennessee Valley.||The slow movement of Fay and the proximity to the forecast area allowed for Fay's impacts on the Peachtree City forecast area to last several days.  Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe County Emergency Management Director reported that a brief EF0 tornado touched down near Plant Scherer, a Georgia Power Plant on the north side of Lake Juliette in northeast Monroe county just west of the Jones county line.  The tornado touched down in a clear cut area, so no damage was observed.  The tornado path length was approximately 1/4 mile long with a maximum path width of 50 yards.  The maximum wind speed in the tornado was estimated to be 70 mph.

Fay continued to move very slowly northeast across the central Florida peninsula, well maintaining its strength along the way, reaching the northeast coast of Florida on August 21st.  At this point, a strengthening subtropical ridge and surface high over the mid-Atlantic region forced Fay to take a sharp westward turn toward the Florida panhandle.  The storm tracked west to west-northwest from the 22nd through the 25th into extreme southeast Louisiana before reaching the western end of the subtropical ridge and an approaching frontal system.  Thus, Fay once again turned back toward the northeast across central Mississippi and central/northern Alabama before finally becoming absorbed into the mean flow and a frontal system located across the Tennessee Valley.||The slow movement of Fay and the proximity to the forecast area allowed for Fay's impacts on the Peachtree City forecast area to last several days.  Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

A cold front was approaching north Georgia as a weak upper-level trough skirted across the northern portion of the state.  This combined with residual tropical moisture and daytime heating resulted in scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms, especially in north Georgia.  While none of the thunderstorms became severe, they produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  Lightning struck at least two homes in Bartow county causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Tribune of Cartersville reported that lightning struck homes on homes on Vigilant Street and Harris street in Atco.  Damage was minimal in both incidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a nearly week long lull from the daily summer convective activity, scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed across the area again.  A weak front, that drifted into the state during the previous weekend, had stalled about halfway across the state.  The front combined with moisture from the extreme outer circulation of hurricane Ike, located near Cuba, and potentially left over residual moisture that drifted over the area from the remnants of hurricane Gustav, provided a favorable environment for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the storms became marginally severe and produced damaging winds.  One storm produced large hail.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed some quarter-sized hail in the extreme southern part of Gwinnett county near Loganville and the Walton county line.  Most of the hail, however, was pea-sized.

A stationary front remained across central Georgia as hurricane Ike continued to churn west through the central Gulf of Mexico.  The large scale circulation around Ike continued to bring Atlantic moisture into Georgia.  This combined with the front to produce scattered to numerous afternoon thunderstorms across central Georgia.  Little to no activity occurred in north Georgia.  While none of the thunderstorms were observed to be severe, frequent cloud-to-ground lightning occurred causing several injuries at a football game in Houston county, just south of Macon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon Telegraph reported that 12 people, nine adults and three teenagers, were injured when lightning struck a goal post on the football field at Bonaire Middle School during a Thursday evening football game.  The athletic director from the opposing school, Warner Robins, was left in critical condition and transferred to a burn center along with three of the other adults.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City and the Jackson County Emergency Management Agency confirmed that an EF1 tornado touched down and caused considerable damage in northeast Jackson county.  The tornado initially touched down very briefly about three miles east-northeast of Arcade near the Brockton Loop before lifting.  Only minor damage to tree limbs was noted in this area.  The tornado then touched down again within the city of Commerce and tracked approximately 1.25 miles through the city before lifting before reaching the Banks county line.  Approximately 40 trees were uprooted or blown over by the tornado, which caused extensive damage to homes in the area.  A total of three mobile homes were destroyed, one by wind, and two others from downed trees, while up to 15 site-built homes sustained minor roof damage.  Three residents of the destroyed mobile homes were treated for injuries.  The maximum path width of the tornado, within the city of Commerce, was determined to be 100 yards.  Maximum wind within the tornado was estimated to be 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay will be remembered from the catastrophic rainfall that it brought to much of Florida as well as being one of the longer lived tropical systems to affect the U.S.  Tropical Storm Fay formed from a tropical wave on August 15th along the east coast of Hispaniola.  The storm moved west along the south coast of Cuba before reaching a weakness in the subtropical ridge to its north causing it turn north-northwestward into central Cuba by the 17th.  Fay continued to track north-northwest through the Florida straits and Florida Keys on the 18th before turning northeast toward the Florida peninsula.  The storm made landfall on August 19th in southwest Florida at Cape Romano as a tropical storm with winds of 60 mph.  Fay continued to move very slowly northeast across the central Florida peninsula, well maintaining its strength along the way, reaching the northeast coast of Florida on August 21st.  At this point, a strengthening subtropical ridge and surface high over the mid-Atlantic region forced Fay to take a sharp westward turn toward the Florida panhandle.  The storm tracked west to west-northwest from the 22nd through the 25th into extreme southeast Louisiana before reaching the western end of the subtropical ridge and an approaching frontal system.  Thus, Fay once again turned back toward the northeast across central Mississippi and central/northern Alabama before finally becoming absorbed into the mean flow and a frontal system located across the Tennessee Valley.||The slow movement of Fay and the proximity to the forecast area allowed for Fay's impacts on the Peachtree City forecast area to last several days.  Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

A weak front was drifting south through north Georgia early on September 9th as large hurricane Ike was moving west off the western tip of Cuba.  The large scale circulation around hurricane Ike brought Atlantic moisture into the region.  This moisture combined with the front to allow for scattered showers and thunderstorms, that developed Tuesday afternoon September 8th, to continue overnight and into the early morning hours Wednesday.  Thunderstorms persisted over parts of east central Georgia in the vicinity of the boundary for several hours, especially in Taliaferro county, where flash flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taliaferro County Sheriff's Department observed a flash flood along a stream that flows into Lick Creek, approximately 1.7 miles northeast of Crawfordville.  The stream washed out a portion of Old Sandy Road, a dirt and rock road.  Severe erosion occurred around the drainage culvert pipe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As tropical storm Fay began its west track across north Flordia, the outer most rain bands and general circulation brought strong gusty winds to the southern parts of central Georgia.  Here are some of the impacts of this first day's impact of tropical storm Fay on the area.||The Toombs County 911 Center reported that three trees were blown down by strong winds associated with the northern outer rain bands associated with Tropical Storm Fay.  Two trees were also blown down in Emanuel county as well.||The Emanuel County 911 Center reported that two trees were blown down by strong winds associated with the outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County 911 Center reported that three trees were blown down by strong winds associated with the northern outer rain bands associated with Tropical Storm Fay.  Two trees were also blown down in Emanuel county as well.||The Emanuel County 911 Center reported that two trees were blown down by strong winds associated with the outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay.

Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

After a nearly week long lull from the daily summer convective activity, scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed across the area again.  A weak front, that drifted into the state during the previous weekend, had stalled about halfway across the state.  The front combined with moisture from the extreme outer circulation of hurricane Ike, located near Cuba, and potentially left over residual moisture that drifted over the area from the remnants of hurricane Gustav, provided a favorable environment for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the storms became marginally severe and produced damaging winds.  One storm produced large hail.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that approximately 25 trees were down in the far eastern portion of the county, about 10 miles east of Canton between Free Home and Buffington.  Some of the trees were very large oaks.  Most of the trees were down in a two-mile wide square area around the Holbrook Camp ground near the Forsyth county line.  Two campers at this camp ground were completely destroyed when two very large oak trees fell on them.  No injuries were reported.

Tropical Storm Fay will be remembered from the catastrophic rainfall that it brought to much of Florida as well as being one of the longer lived tropical systems to affect the U.S.  Tropical Storm Fay formed from a tropical wave on August 15th along the east coast of Hispaniola.  The storm moved west along the south coast of Cuba before reaching a weakness in the subtropical ridge to its north causing it turn north-northwestward into central Cuba by the 17th.  Fay continued to track north-northwest through the Florida straits and Florida Keys on the 18th before turning northeast toward the Florida peninsula.  The storm made landfall on August 19th in southwest Florida at Cape Romano as a tropical storm with winds of 60 mph.  Fay continued to move very slowly northeast across the central Florida peninsula, well maintaining its strength along the way, reaching the northeast coast of Florida on August 21st.  At this point, a strengthening subtropical ridge and surface high over the mid-Atlantic region forced Fay to take a sharp westward turn toward the Florida panhandle.  The storm tracked west to west-northwest from the 22nd through the 25th into extreme southeast Louisiana before reaching the western end of the subtropical ridge and an approaching frontal system.  Thus, Fay once again turned back toward the northeast across central Mississippi and central/northern Alabama before finally becoming absorbed into the mean flow and a frontal system located across the Tennessee Valley.||The slow movement of Fay and the proximity to the forecast area allowed for Fay's impacts on the Peachtree City forecast area to last several days.  Outer rain bands affected the southern parts of the forecast area as early as the 20th.  Outer rain bands continued to affect the southern counties as Fay tracked slowly west through the Florida Panhandle the 21st, 22nd, and 23rd.  Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

This was the second day of significant effects from tropical storm Fay on the north and central Georgia area as the storm drifted slowly west across the Florida panhandle into far southern Alabama.  The outer rain bands brought gusty winds and heavy rain to the far southern and southwestern part of the forecast area.  The strong winds and rain resulted in several trees and some power lines being blown down in areas from Columbus, to Americus and Cordele.  Rainfall of three to four inches fell in the southwest counties from Stewart to Crisp, with two to three inch rainfall amounts further north toward Muscogee, Marion, and Schley counties.  Since the area overall was in a continuing drought condition, no flooding was reported within the Peachtree City forecast area.||Here are some of the specific wind damage reports that were received from the southwestern counties, all a result of winds associated with the outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay.||The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that a few trees were blown down in the Columbus area.  A few of these landed on power lines.  About 250 people were left without power.||The Crisp County Emergency Management Director reported that wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph for much of the previous 24 hour period combined with persistent moderate rain had resulted in up to 15 trees being blown down across the county.||The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that persistent strong and gusty winds combined with a steady moderate to heavy rain had resulted in up to 30 trees being blown down across the county during the previous 24 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Emergency Management Director reported that winds gusts ot 30 to 35 mph for much of the previous 24 hour period had resulted in up to 15 trees being blown down across the county.

After a nearly week long lull from the daily summer convective activity, scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed across the area again.  A weak front, that drifted into the state during the previous weekend, had stalled about halfway across the state.  The front combined with moisture from the extreme outer circulation of hurricane Ike, located near Cuba, and potentially left over residual moisture that drifted over the area from the remnants of hurricane Gustav, provided a favorable environment for scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the storms became marginally severe and produced damaging winds.  One storm produced large hail.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was also observed and there were several lightning-related house fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County Emergency Management Director reported that four trees were down at various locations around the city of Jasper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Towns County Emergency Management Director reported that flash flooding was observed along the Hiawassee River just west of Georgia Highway 75 south of Hiawassee in the Enchanted Valley Resort trailer park.  The river reached a stage of 10.0 feet approximately one mile downstream from the USGS stream gage on Riverside Drive.  The primary impact of the flooding was the wash up of debris into the backyards of several trailer homes.  Minor flood damage occurred to the lower portions of some storage sheds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia and the Hall County Emergency Management Agency confirmed that a second tornado had touched down in Hall county.  This was an EF1 tornado and occurred in the southwest part of the county.  The tornado was broken into two segments, the first 3/4 mile long and the second 2.25 miles long.  The tornado first touched down about one mile southwest of Oakwood and traveled on the ground for approximately 3/4 mile before lifting.  Within this segment of the tornado 20 to 30 trees were knocked down in the area.  Up to 10 homes in the area sustained minor roof damage.  Oakwood Elementary School also suffered minor damage.  Maximum path width for this segment of the tornado was 100 yards.  About five minutes later the tornado touched down again, this time approximately 4 3/4 mile southwest of Gainesville.  This time the tornado was on the ground for 2.25 miles with a maximum path width of 200 yards.  Within this segment of the tornado, dozens of trees were uprooted or blown over.  Up to 15 homes in the El Rancho Estates Subdivision suffered at least minor roof damage from the downed trees and wind.  Lyman Elementary School also sustained minor damage.  Maximum wind for this tornado was estimated to be 90 mph.

Some of these produced gusty winds and a few trees were blown down in the far southeastern counties, namely Toombs with three downed trees and Emanuel with two downed trees.  While no tornadoes occurred at this point, tornado warnings were issued for several counties in the south central and southeast part of the forecast area.  Widespread heavy rain also affected the southwest part of the forecast area on the 23rd as the system tracked west across the Florida Panhandle.  As Fay stalled across southeast Louisiana on the 24th and began its move back to the northeast on the 25th, outer rain bands and thunderstorms started to move back into the area from the southwest during the afternoon of the 24th.  Widespread heavy rain followed on the 25th and 26th, especially across the northwest half of the forecast area.  As the remnant center of circulation moved into northern Alabama on the 26th, spiral bands of rotating thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia.  Six confirmed tornado touchdowns were observed in north central and northeast Georgia as a result of Fay.  These were the first tropical storm-related tornadoes to affect the Peachtree City forecast area since Hurricane Katrina on August 29, 2008.  No lives were lost, but three injuries occurred when one of the associated tornadoes destroyed mobile homes in Jackson county.  Two schools and dozens of homes were damaged in Hall county from other Fay associated tornadoes.  Estimated combined damages for the five days that tropical storm Fay affected the north and central Georgia area were $1.89 million.

The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Banks County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were down in a small area northeast of Lula.  Minor damage was reported to a home from downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia. In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties. Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia and the Wilkes County Emergency Management Director confirmed that an EF0 tornado touched down in northeast Wilkes county about three miles northwest of Tignall near the intersection of Bunch Road and Mallorysville Road.  The tornado path length was determined to be around five miles with the tornado lifting at a point approximately three miles northeast of Norman, or near Henry Hill Road.  A swath of trees were downed all along the entire track of the tornado.  An anchored mobile home along Georgia Highway 17 at Boyd Road was blown six feet of its foundation.  Another home along Georgia Highway 17 lost several shingles from its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning soundings indicated a moist and unstable airmass over the local area with low wind shear and a light southwest steering flow. The precipitable water was 1.76 inches at Jacksonville with around 3000 J/kg of CAPE and a lifted index of -6 degrees Celcius. An upper level low pressure system approached the eastern seaboard during the afternoon which pushed a surface trough over Georgia. This trough in concert with the sea breezes sparked numerous severe storms across the local forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed on Interstate 96 at mile marker 21 between Woodbine and Dover Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms on the 9th caused scattered reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convective gust of 77 mph was recorded by the ASOS at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of wind damage from straight line winds across much of south Georgia during the evening of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down in the southeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low continued to deepen across the northeast U.S. and was digging southward into the southeast United States  A strong cold front was moving south from the Ohio Valley toward the southeast United States.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in Alabama during the early afternoon and expanded and spread eastward into western Georgia during the mid-afternoon.  Many of these storms produced damaging wind and large hail.  An expansive outflow boundary spread eastward across the state in advance of the thunderstorms causing additional thunderstorms to develop further eastward.  However, thunderstorms decreased in coverage as they spread further east and all of the activity had ended by 10 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County 911 Center reported that at least two dozen trees and several power lines were down in the northern portion of the county.  At least five roads were blocked from downed trees. One tree was down on a home causing significant damage to the structure.  Another tree was down on a 500 gallon propane tank causing the tank to leak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north Georgia early on May 10th with an active northwest flow aloft.  Meanwhile...a vigorous short wave aloft was approaching the area from the southern plains.  The stationary front provided the focus for two rounds of showers and thunderstorms, one early in the morning on the 10th and another in the afternoon.  The activity tracked east-southeast with the upper flow aloft, mainly across north Georgia during the early morning and across central Georgia during the afternoon.  An isolated strong supercell also tracked across the southern part of central Georgia during the evening.  After a lull of convective activity for about four hours, intense multicell thunderstorms tracked into the area from Alabama after midnight and before dawn on the 11th.  As these thunderstorms tracked across west central and central Georgia, 15 tornadoes were identified by subsequent surveys making this the most significant tornado outbreak to affect the area since the Katrina-associated tornadoes on August 29, 2005.  Millions of dollars of property damage were reported as many homes were destroyed from these tornadoes from the western and southern suburbs of Atlanta southeastward across Macon, Dublin, and other counties in east central and southeast Georgia.  Many of these counties were eligible for disaster assistance from the federal government.||In addition to the tornadoes and thunderstorm winds that caused extensive damage in dozens of counties across north and central Georgia during the early morning hours of May 11th, strong gradient winds developed on the back side of the strong cold front that moved through the area as low pressure intensified across the mid-Atlantic region.  The strong winds combined with wet ground resulted in dozens of trees being blown down in some north Georgia counties.  There were also two deaths as a result of downed trees in Barrow and Gwinnett county, all non-thunderstorm-related winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale County 911 Center relayed reports of penny-sized hail from southern Rockdale county, generally from near Union Springs along the Henry county line to about six miles southwest of Conyers.  Radar supported that the hail continued into the southern part of Conyers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front continued to approach Georgia from the northwest.  A cutoff upper low was sinking south into the mid-Atlantic region.  These features combined with typical July heat and humidity across the southeast resulted in scattered to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.  The activity was most numerous in the Atlanta metropolitan area and locations east and south of Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck and set a house on fire just north of Thomaston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on the 29th produced some reports of wind damage over portions of southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door type cold front was moving southwest through north and central Georgia.  A deep upper trough was located along the east coast with an active northwest flow continuing into the southeast United States.  An area of thunderstorms developed along the southwestward moving front during the early afternoon and propagated southward along the front through much of east central Georgia.  The thunderstorms merged into a complex of thunderstorms as the afternoon progressed and increased in areal coverage.  The storms produced large hail and damaging downburst winds, especially across the eastern counties of middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County Emergency Management Director reported that numerous trees and power lines were down in the Thompsons Mill area, especially along Interstate-85.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damage to trees, power lines, and several structures across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds snapped power poles and trees.  There were trees on top of several homes and across county and secondary roads.  Uprooted trees knocked holes in the roof of the United Methodist Church in Clyattville.  A roof was lifted off a home on Johnson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked through the upstate of South Carolina and given adequate instability and strong wind fields aloft, a few thunderstorms developed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported a pine tree branch that was six inches in diameter and thirty feet long fell and dented the roof of a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough was sweeping from the Ohio Valley into the southeast U.S.  An unusually strong mid-summer cold front accompanied the upper trough.  These features combined with a moist tropical air mass present over the region resulted in the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the heat of the afternoon and early evening.  Many of these thunderstorms became severe and damaging straight-line and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that a couple of large trees were down across a highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front was located across north Georgia.  A weak trough was moving from the Ohio Valley into the mid-Atlantic and southeast United States.  A strong Bermuda high was present off the southeast United States coast.  This was keeping a warm, moist, and unstable air mass over the region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed across mainly central Georgia where moisture content was higher and the air mass was more unstable.  A few of these became severe, with hail being the primary element.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of large hail were received from the public across northern Houston county.  Quarter to golf ball-sized hail fell near and just northeast through east of Centerville and penny to quarter-sized hail fell in the Warner Robins area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low continued to deepen across the northeast U.S. and was digging southward into the southeast United States  A strong cold front was moving south from the Ohio Valley toward the southeast United States.  Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in Alabama during the early afternoon and expanded and spread eastward into western Georgia during the mid-afternoon.  Many of these storms produced damaging wind and large hail.  An expansive outflow boundary spread eastward across the state in advance of the thunderstorms causing additional thunderstorms to develop further eastward.  However, thunderstorms decreased in coverage as they spread further east and all of the activity had ended by 10 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that several homes sustained minor roof damage from strong thunderstorm winds.  In addition, several street, highway signs, and even some traffic signals were blown down by strong winds.  The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that at least ten trees were down in the south central part of the county from Columbus into the northern part of Fort Benning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system intensified as it moved toward the southeastern states on Saturday Night May 10th and Sunday, May 11th. The combination of warm unstable air and strong wind shear increased the potential for severe thunderstorms and tornadoes across much of the region with a Moderate Risk area issued by the Storms Prediction Center for Sunday. An upper jet axis moved across central Georgia with vertical lift along and north of the jet axis leading to widespread convection over much of southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coffee County Emergency Manager reported trees blown down in the town of Pridgen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into a moist and unstable atmosphere resulting in scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported a downed power line west of Rincon, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to move northeast into central and northern Alabama on the 26th, then finally into eastern Tennessee on the 27th.  This was the day when north and central Georgia experienced its maximum effects from tropical storm Fay.  The outer rain bands of tropical storm Fay spawned six tornadoes across north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  The majority of these affected northeast and east central Georgia.  Three injuries were reported in Jackson county as a result of one of these tornadoes and damage was reported to a shopping center and an elementary school in Hall county.  In addition, widespread heavy rain affected much of north and central Georgia with flash flooding reported in several northeast Georgia mountain counties.  Storm total rainfall from Fay was as high as seven to eight inches across the northeast Georgia mountain counties of White, Lumpkin, Union, and Towns, as well as in adjacent Hall county.  Similar rainfall totals were observed in parts of west central Georgia south and southeast of Columbus, although most of this occurred during the first passage of Fay to the south through the Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hall County 911 Center reported that there was a gradual inundation of creeks and small streams across the northern portion of the county as a result of heavy rain throughout the past 24-36 hours as the remnants of tropical storm Fay continued to affect the area.  No significant damage was reported from this general low-land and creek/small stream flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in combination with the sea breeze in a deep tropical environment, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms and localized flooding across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported a tree down on a power line on Mary Lou Drive, 1 mile east of Hinesville, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in the lee of the Appalachians in combination with the sea breeze resulted in scattered severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report was received of a tree and numerous 4 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs down along Countryside Drive, 2 miles west-southwest of Blichton, Georgia. Slightly larger than quarter size hail fell for 15 minutes as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in combination with the sea breeze in a deep tropical environment, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms and localized flooding across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported portions of Factory Street was flooded, including adjacent yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The sea breeze pushed inland into a highly unstable environment, resulting in a couple isolated severe thunderstorms over southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported by an employee of a restaurant in Stilson, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system intensified as it moved toward the southeastern states on Saturday Night May 10th and Sunday, May 11th. The combination of warm unstable air and strong wind shear increased the potential for severe thunderstorms and tornadoes across much of the region with a Moderate Risk area issued by the Storms Prediction Center for Sunday. An upper jet axis moved across central Georgia with vertical lift along and north of the jet axis leading to widespread convection over much of southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Post Office Reported scattered trees reported down in the vicinity of Denton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system intensified as it moved toward the southeastern states on Saturday Night May 10th and Sunday, May 11th. The combination of warm unstable air and strong wind shear increased the potential for severe thunderstorms and tornadoes across much of the region with a Moderate Risk area issued by the Storms Prediction Center for Sunday. An upper jet axis moved across central Georgia with vertical lift along and north of the jet axis leading to widespread convection over much of southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Hazlehurst by Jeff Davis County 911.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare July cold front was approaching Georgia from the northwest.  A weak upper-level trough accompanied the front.  This combined with typical July afternoon heat and humidity set the stage for scattered thunderstorms.  A couple became severe and produced damaging downburst winds.  Heavy rain also fell across some of the same areas that had experienced flooding during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst resulted in a dozen trees being blown down in the area just west and south of Jasper, especially near Georgia Highway 53, where a large tree fell on and destroyed the deck of a home.  Just south of Jasper, a large oak tree fell on a home causing damage to that structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong cold front and upper-level trough continued to approach Georgia from the northwest as strong low pressure remained over the northeast United States.  This combined with ample daytime heating and moisture produced scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  Several of the storms became severe and produced penny to quarter-sized hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail in the far southwest portion of Toombs county, southwest of Cedar Crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered pulse storms over the Midlands and CSRA produced some wind damage and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported penny size hail on hwy 104.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough was moving from the mid-south toward the southeast U.S. early on the 26th.  A strong cold front accompanied the upper system.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed after midnight on the 25th across Mississippi and Alabama and reached the Georgia/Alabama border around 5 am EST.  The line of thunderstorms intensified and bowed out just as it was moving in Georgia during the early morning hours.  Wind gusts in excess 60 mph affected many counties as these thunderstorms rolled through the area during the early morning hours, causing extensive wind damage to trees, power lines, and some structures from the west and northwest side of Atlanta toward the Alabama border. In addition, two tornadoes, one an EF3, developed along the stronger part of the line as it moved through Carroll county before daybreak causing extensive damage along their paths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Schley County 911 Center reported that several trees were blown down across Georgia Highway 26 northeast of Ellaville and also south of Ellaville on U.S. Highway 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Dolly that moved inland in the Lower Rio Grande Valley of south Texas, tracked around a large subtropical high centered over north Texas and was drifting across north Georgia.  The deep tropical moisture from the remnants of the hurricane combined with cold air aloft in an active northwest flow on the east side of the southern plains subtropical ridge to result in the development of numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  While the moist adiabatic atmosphere keep most thunderstorms below severe limits, flash flooding in the southwest part of the Atlanta metropolitan area became an issue during the evening as thunderstorms trained over the same areas for several hours.  Four to five inches of rain in Fulton and Douglas county southwest of Atlanta was common during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union City Police Department reported that lightning struck and set a tree on fire behind a mobile home in the 7000 block of Goodson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across north Georgia early on May 10th with an active northwest flow aloft.  Meanwhile...a vigorous short wave aloft was approaching the area from the southern plains.  The stationary front provided the focus for two rounds of showers and thunderstorms, one early in the morning on the 10th and another in the afternoon.  The activity tracked east-southeast with the upper flow aloft, mainly across north Georgia during the early morning and across central Georgia during the afternoon.  An isolated strong supercell also tracked across the southern part of central Georgia during the evening.  After a lull of convective activity for about four hours, intense multicell thunderstorms tracked into the area from Alabama after midnight and before dawn on the 11th.  As these thunderstorms tracked across west central and central Georgia, 15 tornadoes were identified by subsequent surveys making this the most significant tornado outbreak to affect the area since the Katrina-associated tornadoes on August 29, 2005.  Millions of dollars of property damage were reported as many homes were destroyed from these tornadoes from the western and southern suburbs of Atlanta southeastward across Macon, Dublin, and other counties in east central and southeast Georgia.  Many of these counties were eligible for disaster assistance from the federal government.||In addition to the tornadoes and thunderstorm winds that caused extensive damage in dozens of counties across north and central Georgia during the early morning hours of May 11th, strong gradient winds developed on the back side of the strong cold front that moved through the area as low pressure intensified across the mid-Atlantic region.  The strong winds combined with wet ground resulted in dozens of trees being blown down in some north Georgia counties.  There were also two deaths as a result of downed trees in Barrow and Gwinnett county, all non-thunderstorm-related winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that yet another tornado touched down and skirted across the far northern part of Laurens county.  This tornado was caused by a separate circulation, although within the same thunderstorm complex, that spawned the EF2 tornado slightly further southwest in north central Laurens county that resulted in two deaths and two injuries.  This tornado touched down as an EF1 tornado in extreme northern Laurens county, approximately one mile west of Tuckers Crossroads.  Near Tuckers Crossroads, one mobile home was destroyed and a manufactured home was pushed off its foundation.  A truck was also moved 30 feet from its original location.  The tornado continued on its eastward track into Johnson county, inflicting more damage before finally lifting just northeast of Wrightsville.  Many trees were blown down along the path of the tornado.  The entire path length of this tornado was 10 miles, but only 1 2/3 mile was within Laurens county.  The maximum path width was estimated to be 1/3 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed southward through southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia and interacted with the sea breeze to produce scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slightly larger than quarter size hail was reported in Savannah, Georgia. The hail resulted in a broken windshield of a car on Burroughs Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system intensified as it moved toward the southeastern states on Saturday Night May 10th and Sunday, May 11th. The combination of warm unstable air and strong wind shear increased the potential for severe thunderstorms and tornadoes across much of the region with a Moderate Risk area issued by the Storms Prediction Center for Sunday. An upper jet axis moved across central Georgia with vertical lift along and north of the jet axis leading to widespread convection over much of southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Appling County Emergency Management Agency reported dime to quarter size hail near Sellers Road and U. S. 1 six miles north of Baxley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of pre-frontal storms first began to impact southeast Georgia before dawn which produced widespread wind damage. Later during the afternoon, large hail storms developed as an upper level short wave trough traversed an unstable airmass with a quasi-stationary front extending near the Florida-Georgia state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter about 4 miles west-southwest of Statenville reported half dollar size hail and 0.72 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in combination with the sea breeze and outflow boundaries from earlier convection, resulted in scattered severe thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported penny size hail 5 miles east-northeast of Newington, Georgia.
